metadata.summary: Two-phase new-hire progression: Contribute (days 31-60, first owned project) and Execute (days 61-90, independent delivery). Each phase ends with checkpoints anchored to concrete artifacts.

content.title: 60-90 Day Onboarding Phases
content.summary: A two-phase framework for structured new-hire progression after the initial 30-day learning phase: Contribute (days 31-60, first owned project, active collaboration) and Execute (days 61-90, independent delivery, measurable results). Each phase ends with a milestone checkpoint anchored to a concrete artifact, not a self-reported activity.

why.p: After the 30-day phase, new hires without explicit milestones plateau or drift below capacity. Separating Contribute from Execute sets a clear intermediate bar: the hire must ship something before being evaluated on independence. The day-45 skip-level and day-60 review catch misalignment mid-ramp when correction is still low-cost; discovering it at the 90-day formal review is too late.

list.item: A hire has cleared 30-day learning criteria and needs explicit Contribute (31-60) and Execute (61-90) milestones.
Manager wants a structured second-and-third-month plan separate from the broad 30-60-90 framework.
Roles with ramp quotas or feature ownership (sales, engineering) where deliverables land in this window.
Preparing a defensible 90-day review with an artifact trail.
Hire is still failing day-30 criteria — resolve or restart the cycle first.
Roles with multi-quarter ramps (Enterprise AE, ML researcher) — extend phases; do not compress.
Senior autonomous hires who define their own deliverables.

rule.statement: The Contribute phase ends when the hire has owned and completed at least one project end-to-end; "participating" or "contributing to" a team project does not satisfy this criterion.
rule.rationale: End-to-end ownership reveals planning, execution, communication, and handoff capability. Participation in a team project reveals only the ability to execute a defined slice; it does not demonstrate the full delivery loop that the Execute phase depends on.

section.name: projects
section.p: Own first project end-to-end. Scope must be achievable within weeks 5-8 — not so small it is trivial, not so large it requires extensions. Project selection is a manager decision made at the day-30 review.

section.name: collaboration
section.p: Contribute actively in meetings — not just attend. Contribution is measurable: made a decision, raised a risk, proposed a solution. Meeting presence without contribution is not a milestone completion criterion.

section.name: relationships
section.p: Build cross-functional connections beyond the immediate team. Identify 3-5 stakeholders in adjacent teams and schedule introductions in weeks 5-6. Goal: the hire knows who to contact for decisions outside their team boundary before the day-60 review.

section.name: feedback
section.p: Receive feedback from at least two sources (manager and peer or cross-functional stakeholder) and demonstrate acting on it. "Acting on it" requires a visible artifact: changed approach, updated doc, adjusted behavior noted by the feedback giver.

rule.statement: Confirm all five day-60 success criteria are met before allowing the hire to enter the Execute phase; missing criteria require a plan for resolution, not a deferral to day-90.
rule.rationale: Deferring unmet 60-day criteria to the 90-day review compresses the Execute phase into the remaining time and produces a rushed, low-evidence review. Addressing gaps at day 60 while there is still 30 days of execution time is the correct intervention point.

list.item: Completed first project successfully (artifact exists: PR merged, doc published, milestone delivered).
Receives positive feedback from team (at least one unsolicited or structured peer feedback).
Works with minimal supervision on familiar tasks within role scope.
Demonstrates core job competencies at the level expected for the role and level.
Has documented goals for days 61-90 in a shared artifact, agreed with manager.

antipattern.description: Defining "contributing" as meeting attendance or activity counts rather than outcome delivery.
antipattern.reason: Activity-based milestones (attended 10 standups, joined 3 cross-team meetings) create false completion signals. A hire who attends all meetings but delivers nothing has not demonstrated contribution — they have demonstrated availability.

antipattern.description: Assigning a project so small it provides no meaningful evidence of delivery capability.
antipattern.reason: A 2-hour task completed in week 5 does not demonstrate the planning, execution, and communication behaviors the phase is designed to reveal. Scope the first project to require 1-2 weeks of sustained effort.

rule.statement: The Execute phase measures independence: the hire must handle most responsibilities without needing guidance; if guidance is still required for familiar tasks at day 90, the hire has not met the ramp bar.
rule.rationale: The 90-day review is the formal transition from onboarding to standard performance management. A hire who still requires frequent guidance at day 90 is on an extended ramp that must be named explicitly and managed differently than the standard onboarding conclusion.

section.name: independence
section.p: Handle responsibilities with minimal guidance. The target is: manager is a resource, not a required step. The hire should seek guidance for novel or high-stakes decisions, not for repeating tasks within their scope.

section.name: delivery
section.p: Complete a major project or milestone — larger in scope than the day-60 project. For engineering: medium-complexity feature. For sales: first closed deal. For PM: shipped feature with usage data. Milestone must be documented before the Execute phase begins.

section.name: initiative
section.p: Proactively identify at least one opportunity, risk, or improvement not assigned by the manager. Initiative evidence: raised the issue in a meeting or 1:1, proposed a solution, and either implemented it or passed it through the appropriate channel.

rule.statement: Include a mentorship milestone only if there is a confirmed newer hire to mentor; never add it as a generic aspiration.
rule.rationale: Generic mentorship milestones at day 90 fail when there are no newer hires; the milestone becomes unfulfillable and the review is forced to waive it, undermining the credibility of the other criteria.

list.item: Meets or exceeds performance expectations on primary job responsibilities.
Works autonomously on 80%+ of tasks without requiring guidance from manager or peers.
Has delivered a major project or milestone with a verifiable artifact.
Has a documented development plan for the next quarter, co-authored with the manager.
Is integrated into team culture — participates in social and professional rituals without prompting.

section.name: check-in-structure

rule.statement: Run a skip-level check-in at day 45; the manager is not the only signal source and may have blind spots the skip-level surfaces.
rule.rationale: Manager-only feedback produces biased signals: managers unconsciously minimize problems they contributed to and amplify successes they credit themselves for. The skip-level provides an independent calibration point mid-ramp.

section.p: Day 45: skip-level check-in (hire meets manager's manager; format is a conversation, not a review). Day 60: manager review with documented outcomes against day-60 success criteria. Day 90: formal performance review with documented outcomes; transition to standard performance management cycle.

section.name: metrics
section.p: 60-day project completion: 100% (tracked by manager). 90-day retention: target above 95% (track voluntary departures in this window). Time to productivity: role-dependent; defined in the plan before Day 1 and assessed by manager at day 90. Onboarding satisfaction: target above 4.0 out of 5 (survey at day 90). 90-day review scores: target above 3.5 out of 5 on primary competency rubric.

antipattern.description: Using the same milestone pace for all hires regardless of role level or prior experience.
antipattern.reason: A senior hire from an adjacent company may complete the Contribute phase in 3 weeks rather than 4-8. Rigidly enforcing the standard cadence frustrates fast ramps and signals the organization cannot adapt to individual capability.

antipattern.description: Skipping the day-45 skip-level because the manager reports the hire is doing well.
antipattern.reason: The skip-level's purpose is precisely to check when the manager reports success. Confident reports from the manager are the highest-risk scenario for a blind-spot review at day 90.

template.purpose: Week-by-week checklist for both phases with 60-day and 90-day success criteria.
